title: Optimizing a Memoized React Component Re-renders Due to Changing Nested Object References

statement.markdown: Write a memoized React component that efficiently re-renders only when the nested object references inside its state change. The component receives a nested object `data` as a prop and maintains its own state `count`. When a new `data` is passed, the component should update its state accordingly.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be an object representing the nested data structure. For example: `{ a: { b: { c: 1 } }, d: { e: 2 } }`
statement.outputFormat: The component should render the updated count.
statement.notes: You should consider using a library like `lodash.isequal` to compare the nested objects.

constraints.description: Maximum depth of the nested object

examples.explanation: The component receives the initial nested object and updates its state accordingly.
The component re-renders with the updated count when a new nested object is passed.

prerequisites: Basic knowledge of React and memoization

hints.text: Use `lodash.isequal` to compare the nested objects.
Consider using a recursive function to traverse the nested object.

editorial.title: Memoized React Component Optimization
editorial.intuition: The key to optimizing the memoized component is to efficiently compare the nested objects.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach would involve checking all nested object properties for changes.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution involves using `lodash.isequal` to compare the nested objects.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a recursive function to traverse the nested object and update the state accordingly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness involves demonstrating that the updated state reflects the changes in the nested object.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run would involve testing the component with various inputs to ensure it behaves as expected.
